title: If Andy Burnham wants to understand England, he should take a taxi
gist: Prospect's Sarah Collins rides along with theatre company Dante or Die's project interviewing taxi drivers across Bradford, Hull, the Isle of Sheppey and Peckham, following the 2025 'Unite the Kingdom' rally and the May 2026 local elections (Reform UK gained ~1,500 council seats while Labour and the Conservatives together lost 2,000+). Drivers from very different backgrounds share the same underlying grievances — cost of living, social isolation, small-boat Channel crossings — but diverge sharply, sometimes into conspiracy theory, on causes and solutions, illustrating a Britain fragmenting into incompatible political realities rather than converging around shared hardship.

insights.claim: Dante or Die co-founder Daphna Attias frames taxi drivers as informal 'focus groups on wheels,' positioned at the frontline of their communities because they absorb unfiltered political and social sentiment from a wide cross-section of daily fares.
insights.speaker: Daphna Attias
insights.so_what: A cheap, real-time qualitative sentiment-gauge methodology — comparable to using cabbies as an informal polling proxy for grassroots political mood between elections.
insights.quote: It feels like taxi drivers are at the frontline of knowing their communities, their streets, and have their fingers on the pulse of what the people in their local area feel.
insights.locator: Or as Daphna Attias, one of the two founders

insights.claim: Dante or Die's taxi-driver interview project was prompted by Tommy Robinson's 'Unite the Kingdom' rally and the wave of English flags that subsequently appeared on lampposts nationwide; the finished documentary will tour England later in 2026 in a repurposed black cab.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Dates the flag phenomenon to a specific triggering event, useful for correlating subsequent polling and election shifts to a traceable political-cultural marker.
insights.locator: Daphna and her co-founder and co-artistic director, Terry O'Donovan

insights.claim: In the May 2026 local elections, Labour and the Conservatives together lost over 2,000 council seats while Reform UK gained almost 1,500; the Greens took overall control of three councils and won their first two directly elected mayoralties, in Lewisham and Hackney.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of two-party collapse and a three-way fragmentation (Reform/Green/incumbent) — a structural read for modeling UK political risk ahead of the next general election.
insights.quote: Between them, Labour and the Conservatives lost over 2,000 council seats, while Reform UK swept up almost 1,500.
insights.locator: Then came the May 2026 local elections

insights.claim: Every taxi driver interviewed for the project, across Bradford, Sheppey, Hull and Peckham, was operating in a local council that ended up controlled by a non-mainstream party after the May 2026 elections.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Mainstream-party collapse is concentrated precisely in the deprived, high-migration areas most exposed to cost-of-living and small-boats grievances — useful for geographically targeting UK political risk.
insights.quote: not a single driver who features in their films, or was interviewed for this article, was operating in a local council controlled by a mainstream party after the May elections
insights.locator: By that point, Daphna and Terry had already interviewed

insights.claim: Across drivers from very different backgrounds (British-Pakistani Muslim women in Bradford, Eastern Europeans in Hull, white British in Sheppey), the same grievances recur — cost of living, social isolation, small-boat crossings — but interpretation of causes and solutions diverges into what the author calls incompatible worldviews.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Shared economic pain is being channeled into opposite political directions by identity/worldview rather than by material interest — a caution against assuming shared hardship converges on shared policy demand.
insights.quote: less differing political persuasions and more utterly incompatible worldviews and versions of reality

themes.theme: Bradford: flags, Islamophobia fears and Reform's council win

insights.claim: In Bradford's May 2026 elections, Reform UK won 29 council seats in an authority home to roughly 170,000 Muslims; the new council leader, Stephen Place, has been accused of posting sexist and Islamophobic content, much of it focused on small-boat asylum seekers.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: A concrete case of Reform's electoral gains translating into leadership figures with documented Islamophobic rhetoric — a direct input for modeling social-cohesion risk in Reform-controlled councils.
insights.quote: Reform won 29 seats on Bradford council, which covers an area that approximately 170,000 Muslims call home
insights.locator: In May, some of her fears were realised

insights.claim: Bradford driver Faiza says English flags in neighbouring Wakefield make her feel unsafe and unwelcome driving alone as a visibly Muslim woman, describing them as intimidating even though she has personally never had a problem driving through areas that display them.
insights.speaker: Faiza
insights.so_what: A ground-level illustration of how symbolic nationalism generates a perceived-safety gap distinct from actual incident rates — relevant to gauging social-cohesion strain independent of crime data.
insights.quote: It’s Wakefield. There’s English flags everywhere. And then there’s me driving a taxi on my own. What am I doing?

insights.claim: Amberine Nawaz runs Pink Ladies, Bradford's only taxi firm exclusively employing Muslim women drivers for women customers; she says business is booming and she is constantly hiring, while separately reporting her daughter has been racially harassed ('curry-muncher') in nearby Skipton.
insights.speaker: Amberine Nawaz
insights.so_what: Identifies a niche, demand-driven women-only/faith-affinity taxi service model with apparently strong unit economics growing out of a safety-and-comfort gap in the mainstream taxi/rideshare market.
insights.quote: My daughter has experienced some racism in Skipton. She has been called a curry-muncher.

themes.theme: Hull: cost of living, immigration blame and conspiracy belief

insights.claim: Hull has one of the UK's highest unemployment rates at 7.5%, and ranks as the fourth most deprived local authority in the country, with both employed and unemployed residents facing poverty.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Concrete deprivation data anchoring why cost-of-living grievance, and its political redirection toward immigration, is especially acute in Hull relative to national averages.
insights.quote: Hull has one of the highest unemployment rates in the country, at 7.5 per cent
insights.locator: He chose the city because it is cheap

insights.claim: Bosnian-refugee-turned-Hull-cabbie Ivan blames immigration for the local housing crisis and views recent small-boat arrivals with suspicion relative to his own 1990s war-refugee experience, even though the Chartered Institute of Housing attributes Britain's housing crisis to social-housing sales, chronic undersupply and outdated planning rules rather than migration.
insights.speaker: Ivan
insights.so_what: A direct example of a perception/data gap — driver-level causal attribution of the housing crisis to immigration diverges from the housing sector's own expert diagnosis, useful for calibrating how much anti-immigration sentiment is evidence-based versus narrative-driven.
insights.quote: according to the Chartered Institute of Housing, there is no clear link between migration and Britain’s housing crisis, which has deeper roots in the selling of social housing, a chronic failure to build new supply and outdated planning rules
insights.locator: He believes that immigration is the main cause

insights.claim: Ivan subscribes to the 'Tartaria' internet conspiracy theory (that a lost advanced civilisation built many old buildings, dubbed the 'QAnon of architecture') and separately believes the UN's 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development is an elite plot to create a totalitarian world order.
insights.speaker: Ivan
insights.so_what: A firsthand data point on how niche internet conspiracy theories have penetrated ordinary working-class political conversation, feeding broader institutional distrust that shapes voting behavior.
insights.quote: he believes, for instance, that the UN’s 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development represents a plot by elites to create a new totalitarian world order
insights.locator: He is a believer in the Tartaria

insights.claim: Despite voicing strong grievances about small boats and immigration, Ivan says he believes UK election outcomes are predetermined regardless of who people vote for, suggesting some anti-establishment-leaning voters have disengaged from believing their vote affects anything.
insights.speaker: Ivan
insights.so_what: Signals a fatalistic non-participation risk within the anti-establishment-leaning electorate — a potential drag on Reform/protest-vote turnout that pure grievance-polling would miss.
insights.quote: I think they have all been chosen before us. So it doesn’t matter.
insights.locator: I ask Ivan whether this issue will decide his vote

themes.theme: Sheppey: 'Reform Island' without a Reform vote

insights.claim: Despite the Isle of Sheppey being dubbed 'Reform Island' as one of the UK's most deprived authorities, cabbie Lawrence is not a Reform voter and rates Boris Johnson and Andy Burnham — not Farage, Starmer or Badenoch — as the only politicians he considers fit for the premiership, while still supporting his neighbours' right to fly English flags as a protest against government inaction on Channel crossings.
insights.speaker: Lawrence
insights.so_what: Complicates the flags-equal-Reform-vote assumption: symbolic nationalism can coexist with rejecting Reform outright, meaning flag prevalence is a noisy proxy for actual voting intent.
insights.quote: Nobody’s put a flag up on my street, but if my neighbours did, I’d put one up. And an Irish flag too.
insights.locator: Lawrence is not a Reform voter

insights.claim: Lawrence attributes declining pub and taxi trade on Sheppey partly to a pandemic-driven shift toward drinking at home, noting supermarket cider (18 cans of Magners for £12 at Morrisons) is far cheaper than pub prices (£5-6 a pint) — a dynamic coinciding with over 2,000 UK pub closures since 2020.
insights.speaker: Lawrence
insights.so_what: A micro-level price-substitution mechanism behind the broader UK pub-closure trend (2,000+ since 2020) — relevant for on-trade versus off-trade alcohol volume shifts and hospitality-sector demand forecasts.
insights.quote: I can go over to Morrisons and I can get 18 Magners for 12 quid... In the pub, a pint of Magners would probably be five or six quid.

themes.theme: Pandemic scarring and multicultural anxiety in London

insights.claim: Post-pandemic UK household disposable income has not yet recovered to end-2019 levels per Joseph Rowntree Foundation data; a Mind charity survey found one in three adults report worsened mental health since the pandemic, and the Campaign to End Loneliness reports loneliness levels, which rose during lockdowns, also remain elevated versus pre-pandemic.
insights.speaker: Sarah Collins
insights.so_what: Three independent data sources converge on the same read — the UK's pandemic economic and mental-health scarring is still unresolved years on, a durable headwind for consumer demand and social cohesion alike.
insights.quote: one in three adults say their mental health has deteriorated since the pandemic
insights.locator: According to data from the Joseph Rowntree Foundation

insights.claim: Southwark (Peckham) driver Frank, a 62-year-old Ghanaian who has driven in London for 20 years, says his finances have deteriorated sharply since Covid — he can no longer save after paying for his car, rent and food — while Labour lost overall control of Southwark council for the first time in 16 years in May 2026 to a Green-Lib coalition.
insights.speaker: Frank
insights.so_what: Pairs individual-level financial deterioration with a concrete electoral outcome (Labour's 16-year Southwark run ending) — a localized case of cost-of-living pain converting into anti-incumbent voting, but toward the Greens rather than Reform.
insights.quote: you pay for the car, you pay for your rent and the feeding, and there’s nothing left. So there’s no savings.
insights.locator: Frank is not the only driver

insights.claim: Frank says some English customers have told him they would leave the country if Nigel Farage became prime minister, which he reads as evidence of broad support for Black communities among English people, even as he otherwise avoids discussing politics with fares out of caution.
insights.speaker: Frank
insights.so_what: A rare data point suggesting a Farage premiership could trigger emigration-intent signaling among some UK residents — worth tracking as a soft indicator of professional-class flight risk.
insights.quote: I have spoken to one or two English people, and they say they won’t live here if [Farage] wins.
insights.locator: Frank remembers a time when he was scared
